original_abstract:
Workplace bullying is a pervasive and destructive phenomenon that negatively impacts both individual employees and organizational efficiency. Characterized by repetitive behaviors such as unreasonable deadlines, excessive monitoring, or the removal of professional responsibilities, bullying can lead to severe emotional distress and a lack of focus among staff. This study explores the relationship between workplace bullying—specifically work-related bullying, personal bullying, and physical intimidation—and its consequences on the quality of work life (QWL).

Utilizing a quantitative research design, data were collected from 178 public service employees within a local government setting in Medan, Indonesia. Assessment tools included the Quality of Work Life scale and the Negative Acts Questionnaire-Revised (NAQ-R), with results analyzed via stepwise regression. The findings revealed that all three dimensions of bullying were significantly and negatively correlated with QWL. Specifically, physical intimidation emerged as the strongest predictor of diminished QWL (β = –0.285), followed by personal bullying (β = –0.245) and work-related bullying (β = –0.210).

The results underscore the critical role of organizational leadership in fostering a safe and supportive work environment. To mitigate these adverse effects, organizations must implement transparent anti-bullying policies, establish behavioral standards, and proactively manage workplace risks. Improving the quality of work life is essential not only for the psychological well-being of employees but also for the long-term stability and productivity of the workforce.
humanized_abstract:
Workplace bullying is a serious and persistent social issue that damages not only employees but also the organization’s operation. Bullying behaviors at the workplace can include a series of non-punitive actions such as forced working hours, unreasonable work requirements, too frequent supervision of work or deprivation of work tasks. Workplace bullying may cause great mental pressure to employees and thus affects the employees’ concentration on work. This research study aimed to investigate the relationship between work-related bullying, personal bullying, physical intimidation and the quality of work life (QWL).

This study employed a quantitative approach by collecting the data from 178 public servants in a local government agency in Medan, Indonesia. The Quality of Work Life (QWL) scale and Negative Acts Questionnaire-Revised (NAQ-R) were used to collect the data. The data were analyzed by employing stepwise regression methods. The results indicated that all three forms of bullying had a significant and negative correlation with the QWL of the public servants. The physical intimidation had the highest correlation with the QWL (β = -0.285), followed by personal bullying (β = -0.245) and work-related bullying (β = -0.210).

The findings highlight the important work that leadership does to ensure that the workplace is a safe and healthy place for everyone. To reduce risks to employees from workplace bullying, organizations need to have clear anti-bullying policies and procedures, a code of conduct and a process to deal with workplace hazards. Improving work life is important for many reasons, including improving the mental health of workers and contributing to workforce sustainability and productivity.
